2015 · ICCV · Benchmarks & Evals

VQA: Visual Question Answering

Turn "answer this free-form question about this image" into a benchmark with a number on it — ~0.25M images, ~0.76M questions, ~10M human answers — and, in the same paper, show how far a model gets by ignoring the image entirely.

Stanislaw Antol*, Aishwarya Agrawal*, Jiasen Lu*, Margaret Mitchell, Dhruv Batra, C. Lawrence Zitnick, Devi Parikh  ·  *equal contribution
venue ICCV 2015 / IJCV citations 6,474 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2015, image captioning was the marquee "vision + language" task — but it had a quiet problem. The authors point out that a coarse, scene-level understanding paired with word n-gram statistics was already enough to produce captions that scored well, which suggested captioning "may not be as 'AI-complete' as desired." Worse, captioning had no clean evaluation: judging a generated sentence against references is itself an open research problem.

The authors wanted a task that was harder to fake and easier to grade. Their criteria for an "AI-complete" task: it should (i) require multi-modal knowledge beyond a single sub-domain, and (ii) have a well-defined, quantitative evaluation metric to track progress. Captioning fails (ii); object detection arguably fails (i).

Visual Question Answering threads the needle. Given an image and a free-form, open-ended natural-language question, produce an accurate natural-language answer. A single dataset can demand fine-grained recognition ("what kind of cheese is on the pizza?"), counting ("how many bikes are there?"), activity recognition ("is this man crying?"), knowledge-base lookup ("is this a vegetarian pizza?"), and commonsense ("does this person have 20/20 vision?"). Crucially, because most answers are one to three words, VQA is amenable to automatic evaluation in a way captioning is not — you can just count how often the system is right.

// how it works

The dataset, the metric, the model

The dataset. VQA is built on 204,721 real images from MS COCO plus a synthetic 50,000-scene "abstract scenes" set (clipart "paperdoll" people and objects, designed to isolate high-level reasoning from low-level perception). Workers were primed with a "stump the smart robot" interface — asked to pose questions a smart robot would likely fail but any human could answer while looking — to bias toward image-dependent, non-generic questions. Three questions were collected per image, and ten answers per question from unique workers, yielding ~0.76M questions and ~10M answers in total. Answers are short: 89.32% are a single word on real images. The task ships in two flavors: open-ended (free response) and multiple-choice (pick from 18 candidates).

The metric. Open-ended answers are graded against the ten human answers with a consensus rule that tolerates disagreement: an answer counts as fully correct once at least three of the ten workers gave it.

accuracy(ans) = min( # humans who gave ans3 , 1) // answers lower-cased, numbers→digits, punctuation & articles stripped; score averaged over all 10 leave-one-out subsets of the annotators

The authors deliberately reject machine-translation metrics like BLEU and ROUGE (answers are too short for n-gram overlap to mean anything, and those metrics correlate poorly with human judgement) and soft embedding metrics like Word2Vec similarity (which would wrongly merge "left" and "right"). The price is exact-string matching, which they acknowledge undercounts synonyms.

The model. To show the dataset is tractable but unsolved, they build a two-channel baseline. A VGGNet image embedding (last hidden layer, ℓ₂-normalized, 4096-dim, frozen) and an LSTM question embedding are each projected to a common 1024-dim space, fused by element-wise multiplication ("late fusion"), and passed through an MLP + softmax over the top K = 1000 answers (which cover 82.67% of train+val answers). The full model is "deeper LSTM Q + norm I." They also run deliberately weak baselines — random, the "yes" prior, per-question-type priors, nearest-neighbor, and crucially blind (question-only) and image-only variants — to measure how much each modality actually contributes.

// what they found

The results

Humans set a high but imperfect ceiling. Shown the question and the image, humans answer 83.30% of real-image questions correctly (87.49% on abstract scenes). Shown only the question, they manage 40.81% — and given a human caption instead of the image, only 57.47%. That caption gap is the paper's cleanest argument: generic descriptions don't carry the information needed to answer specific questions.

Human, free-form (test-standard, real)AllYes/NoOther
Question only (no image)40.8167.6021.22
Question + caption57.4778.9744.41
Question + image83.3095.7772.67

The models tell a more uncomfortable story. The best model reaches 58.16% open-ended / 63.09% multiple-choice on test-standard — far below the 83.30% human mark. But look at the baselines: an image-only model scores just 28.13%, worse than blindly answering "yes" to everything (29.66%). A blind, question-only LSTM scores 48.76% — within ~9 points of the full multimodal model (57.75% on test-dev). In other words, most of the model's apparent competence comes from the question alone; the image adds surprisingly little.

Method (test-dev, real images)Open-endedMult.-choice
prior ("yes")29.6629.66
per Q-type prior37.5439.45
nearest neighbor42.7048.49
image only (I)28.1330.53
blind LSTM Q (no image)48.7654.75
BoW Q + I52.6458.97
deeper LSTM Q + norm I (best)57.7562.70
human (Q + image)83.30—

Per-question-type analysis sharpens the point. For reasoning-heavy types like "Is the…" or "How many…", adding the image barely helps; for scene-level types like "What sport…" it helps a lot. Mapped onto the dataset's MTurk age annotations, the best model "performs as well as a 4.74 year old child," against an average required age of 8.98; its estimated degree of commonsense is 17.35% versus a human 31.23%. The dataset is rich and skewed: questions cluster into a long tail of "What is…", "Is there…", "How many…" types (Figure 3), and 38.37% of real-image questions are yes/no — of which 58.83% are "yes."

// does it hold up?

The eval read

VQA's lasting importance is half the dataset and half the self-diagnosis baked into its own tables. To its credit, the paper ships the blind and image-only baselines that expose its central weakness — most benchmarks of the era would have buried them. But the weakness is real and structural: a question-only model reaches 48.76% while the full multimodal model reaches 57.75%, so a system can look competent while barely using the image. The dataset leaks the answer through the question — "what color is the banana?" is answerable as "yellow," yes/no skews 58.83% "yes" — and the consensus metric rewards predicting the modal answer per question type. This isn't test-set contamination (the test-standard / test-reserve evaluation-server design is actually a solid overfitting guard for its time); it's distributional leakage inside the task itself. That exact failure is what motivated VQA v2 (Goyal et al., 2017), which balances each question with a complementary image yielding a different answer, and a line of "don't just assume, look" work.

Two more things the headline number hides. First, "open-ended" is generous framing: the released models classify over the top 1000 answers (82.67% coverage) and the metric is exact string match, so synonyms like "white"/"off-white" or "happy"/"joyful" are scored wrong — the human ceiling of 83.30% is itself partly an artifact of the matching rule (inter-human agreement is <76% on non-yes/no questions). Second, multiple-choice scores run consistently higher than open-ended (63.09 vs 58.16 for the best model), and later work showed MC can be partly gamed by ranking options without reading the question. The evocative "4.74-year-old" and "17.35% commonsense" figures are derived from perceived MTurk age/commonsense judgements (the two studies correlate only at Pearson 0.58), and the authors themselves caution these are relative, not absolute.

baselines

Strong and honest for grading — random, priors, nearest-neighbor, and (decisively) blind question-only and image-only models. Those blind baselines are the paper's most valuable contribution: they quantify the language-prior shortcut instead of hiding it.

contamination

Not classic test-set leakage — the held-out test-standard/test-reserve server design guards against overfitting. The real leak is internal: question phrasing and answer-class skew let a model shortcut the image.

what's missed

Exact-match over a 1000-answer vocabulary penalizes synonyms and effectively closes the "open-ended" set; yes/no class imbalance inflates accuracy; the metric can't tell whether a right answer came from looking or guessing.

would raise confidence

Balanced complementary image pairs (delivered by v2); a synonym-robust metric; reporting the blind-baseline gap as a headline; grounding/consistency checks that the model attends to the right region.

// bottom line

A field-defining benchmark whose greatest legacy may be that it measured its own shortcut. The 83% human vs 58% model gap launched a decade of multimodal evaluation; the 48.76% blind baseline taught that field its first hard lesson — a high score on a vision-language benchmark can be language with the vision switched off.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. VQA reframed vision+language as a gradable task: free-form question about an image → short answer, scored by human consensus (≥3 of 10 ⇒ correct). It made "does the model understand the image?" a number, where captioning could not.
  2. Its blind baseline is the punchline: a question-only model hits 48.76% vs 57.75% for the full model and 83.30% for humans — proof that language priors, not perception, carry most VQA accuracy. This seeded VQA v2's balanced pairs and the entire "shortcut learning" critique of multimodal benchmarks.
  3. Scale plus a simple, defensible metric (rejecting BLEU/ROUGE for short answers) made VQA the template the modern multimodal-eval lineage — ScienceQA, MathVista, MMMU and beyond — is built on, and the cautionary tale they all cite.
